title: Suffering & Pain
subtitle: What the Bible Says About Suffering
summary: Scripture does not explain away suffering but meets it with honesty, offering the presence of God in pain rather than easy answers, and pointing toward a day when every tear will be wiped away.
body:
The question of suffering is the Bible's most persistent and least resolved. Job loses everything and demands an answer from God. When God finally responds, he does not explain the suffering — he reveals himself [verse:job.38.1-4]. The answer to why is not a proposition but a presence.

The Psalms model what it looks like to suffer honestly before God. My God, my God, why have you forsaken me [verse:ps.22.1]? Jesus himself quotes this psalm from the cross, entering fully into the experience of divine absence [verse:matt.27.46]. If the Son of God can cry out in anguish, then anguish is not a sign of faithlessness.

Paul reframes suffering without minimizing it. Present sufferings are not worth comparing with the glory that will be revealed [verse:rom.8.18]. This is not dismissal but proportion — the pain is real, but it is not the final word. He also testifies that God's grace is sufficient even when the thorn is not removed [verse:2cor.12.9].

Peter tells suffering believers that their trials refine their faith the way fire refines gold [verse:1pet.1.6-7]. This does not make the fire welcome, but it gives it meaning.

The ultimate answer is eschatological. Revelation pictures God dwelling with his people, wiping every tear from their eyes [verse:rev.21.3-4]. Death, mourning, crying, and pain will be no more. Until that day, believers walk through suffering with a God who has entered it himself.

verses.annotation: God responds to Job not with explanation but with revelation of his own wisdom and power.
Present suffering is real but not comparable to the coming glory — pain is not the last word.
The cry of forsakenness — later quoted by Jesus on the cross, validating honest anguish.
God's grace sufficient in weakness — the thorn remains but power is made perfect in it.
Trials as refining fire — painful but purposeful, producing proven faith.
Jesus himself cries out in agony from the cross — God entering human suffering.
The promise of a world without tears, death, or pain — the eschatological hope.

scholars.quote: Clines argues that the book of Job deliberately refuses to provide a theodicy — God's response from the whirlwind redirects the question from 'why do I suffer?' to 'who is God?', which proves to be the more transformative question.

Moo reads Romans 8:18 not as Paul minimizing suffering but as placing it within a narrative arc — the present chapter is painful, but the story has a glorious conclusion that recontextualizes everything.

Goldingay observes that Psalm 22's movement from anguish to praise mirrors the structure of faith itself — suffering is not denied but moved through, and the one who cries out eventually testifies to deliverance.
